turns.text: Hello Heike, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s funny, sitting here, I almost feel like I’m in the wrong chair. Usually, I’m the one on the other side of the desk, flipping through a chart and checking off developmental milestones, trying to put a young mom at ease. But now? I’m the one with the flagged chart, and honestly, it’s humbling—and a little terrifying. 

I’m only nine weeks along, and the exhaustion is… it’s something else. Between the morning sickness—which, let’s be real, is more of an all-day metallic taste in my mouth—and chasing my toddler around while he deals with his own molars, I feel like I’m constantly running on fumes. 

The hardest part is that dual identity. My "nurse brain" keeps analyzing every twinge, calculating the genetic risks, and seeing the potential complications play out like a film reel. I want to talk to my midwife about the NIPT and the statistical realities, but then I catch myself wanting to stop, grab her hand, and just have her tell me that this baby is going to be okay. It’s hard to switch that off. I’m trying to stay present, but the anxiety of being forty-two makes it difficult to just… breathe and be a mom instead of a clinician. I suppose I'm still figuring out how to be a patient again.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Honestly, I think I was a little arrogant about it. I spent years navigating the healthcare system from the inside, so I figured I’d just glide through. I expected that since I knew the language—the shorthand, the procedures, the protocols—I’d get a more "peer-to-peer" level of care. I thought I’d be able to breeze past the generic pamphlets and get straight to the nuanced clinical discussions.

In reality? It’s been much more isolating than I anticipated. Being a nurse doesn't actually shield you from the vulnerability of being a patient; if anything, it makes it sharper. When I sit in that exam room, my midwife sees my age, sees the "medium risk" label, and naturally, her tone shifts. She explains things to me in a way that’s technically accurate but feels like she’s reciting a script. And I catch myself nodding along, even when I’m screaming inside, "I know the pathology, just tell me if my baby is okay." 

I wanted guidance that felt like a partnership, but instead, I feel like I'm constantly performing "the calm, informed professional" while my nerves are absolutely shredded. I didn't realize how much I needed someone to look past the charts and just acknowledge the fear of a forty-two-year-old mother-to-be, rather than just treating my pregnancy like a data set.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I suppose I expected to feel more… grounded. I’ve seen so many parents navigate this, and I’ve spent my career acting as an anchor for them. I thought that experience would translate into a sort of quiet confidence, like I had already "rehearsed" the emotional landscape of pregnancy. I thought I’d be able to keep the anxiety at arm's length because I understand the science behind it.

The reality, though, is that I feel completely unmoored. It’s one thing to explain the physiology of a pregnancy to a worried mother in a clinic; it’s quite another to feel the visceral, primitive fear for your own child. My professional expertise feels like a double-edged sword—it’s not acting as a shield; it’s acting as a magnifying glass. Every time I experience a new symptom, I’m not just feeling it, I’m diagnosing it, and my mind immediately jumps to the "what-ifs." 

I thought I would be more prepared for the transition back to being the one in the patient gown, but I’m finding that I’m just as fragile as anyone else. There’s a sharp disconnect between the calm, competent nurse I am at work and the worried, exhausted woman staring out her porch window at 6:00 AM, just hoping for a healthy heartbeat.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

What’s missing is a space where I don’t have to be the "expert." Everyone around me—my colleagues, even my friends—they all treat me like this fountain of knowledge. When I mention feeling scared about the genetic screening, they say, "Well, you know the stats better than anyone, Heike, you’re fine." And while that’s true, it feels like they’re using my degree as a wall to keep me from needing to be comforted. It’s almost like I’m not allowed to be a frightened mother because I’m also a nurse.

Professionally, I crave someone to just sit with me and acknowledge that being forty-two is *hard*. I don't need another lecture on chromosomal abnormalities; I need someone to validate the exhaustion and the fact that I am terrified of losing this pregnancy.

Even at home, it’s tricky. My husband is wonderful, but he’s already in "problem-solving" mode, trying to fix the logistics—childcare, work schedules, appointments. He’s my partner in the "doing," but sometimes I just need him to stop doing and just be there with me in the worry. I think I’m lonely in my own expertise. I’m surrounded by people who trust my brain so much that they’ve forgotten I still have a heart that’s racing, too.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

Before I started, I expected to find a wealth of practical, empowering information—the kind of stuff that would help me feel in control. I thought I’d find data-driven resources that respected my intelligence but also offered a bit of warmth. 

Instead, it’s been a minefield. The apps and websites I’ve stumbled upon are polarized: they’re either patronizing, with all the cutesy, reductive "your baby is the size of a poppy seed" talk that makes my eyes roll, or they’re alarmist, clinical databases that lead me straight down a rabbit hole of worst-case scenarios. 

When I look up something as simple as fatigue or spotting, the search results feel like a diagnostic trap. I’m either reading blog posts that tell me to "trust my intuition" and "breathe through the journey"—which feels like toxic positivity when I’m staring at a medium-risk chart—or I’m reading raw medical journals that treat pregnancy like a pathology to be managed. There’s almost nothing in between. 

I’m left with a choice: I can be treated like a child who needs a sticker for making it through the week, or I can be treated like a collection of risk factors. Neither of those versions of "information" actually makes me feel seen or comforted as a woman who just wants her baby to be healthy.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap is in the *nuance of the "medium risk" label*. Digital sources are binary; they’re either scaring the hell out of you with catastrophic stories on forums or they’re sugarcoating reality with vague, overly optimistic language that ignores the physiological hurdles of a forty-two-year-old body. They lack the ability to look at my specific history—my lifestyle, my health, my previous pregnancy—and synthesize that into a coherent picture.

My healthcare provider, conversely, is governed by clinical efficiency. When I bring up the things I’ve read, she tends to dismiss them as "internet noise," which I understand—she has protocols to follow. But the gap is that she doesn't fill the void those sources leave. She speaks in probabilities and ranges, which is technically what I should want as a nurse, but it feels devoid of the human context I’m actually living.

There’s no space for the "gray" areas. I feel like I’m constantly triangulating between an app telling me everything is just "a beautiful journey" and a midwife telling me "here are your percentages." Nobody is saying, "Heike, the data shows this, but here is how we are going to look after *you* as a person, specifically." I’m left to bridge that gap myself, and frankly, I’m just too tired for that kind of mental gymnastics.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

My age, forty-two, isn't exactly a disease, but it is treated like one the second you walk through the door. Before I started this, I honestly expected some kind of proactive "pre-conception counseling" that felt, I don’t know, *personalized*. I thought there would be a conversation about how my specific body might handle the added strain, maybe a map of what to look out for, or even just someone saying, "We’re going to be your team for this." 

Instead, it felt like I was just handed a generic list of "advanced maternal age" warnings. The preparation was basically a series of "if this happens, call us, otherwise wait for your appointment" directives. It was all reactive. When I tried to bring up my concerns—about the specific physiological toll of being older and how that might complicate my energy levels or my recovery—the response felt like it was plucked from a standard clinical template. 

It wasn't that they were mean; they were just efficient. They checked the boxes. They verified I was on the right prenatal vitamins and looked at my blood pressure, but they didn’t acknowledge the *transition*. It’s like they assume that because I’m a nurse, I don’t need the hand-holding, or worse, that because I’m "advanced maternal age," the focus should solely be on the risks rather than my actual quality of life. I felt like I was being processed, not prepared.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I suppose I pictured us being a united front—a team strategizing together, sitting down with a cup of coffee to talk about the physical toll and the emotional shifts. I imagined us reading the same books or at least being on the same page about the "what-ifs." I wanted that shared intimacy of preparing for a life that we both desperately wanted.

In reality, my husband’s involvement has been much more… functional. He’s shifted into "protector" mode, which I know comes from a place of love, but it manifests as him trying to solve everything. If I mention that I’m struggling with the morning sickness, he’s immediately on the phone looking for the best crackers or adjusting the grocery list. He’s taking over the heavy lifting with our toddler and managing the household, which I am profoundly grateful for, but it feels like he’s trying to "manage" the pregnancy out of existence.

He thinks that by keeping everything running smoothly, he’s helping me, but it creates this weird distance. When I try to tell him I’m scared about the genetic screening, he just tells me the odds are in our favor and that we’re doing everything right. He wants to keep things light and logical because he can’t stand to see me anxious. It leaves me feeling a bit alone in the messy, middle-of-the-night parts where you can't just fix things with a better diet or a calmer schedule. He’s doing his best, but sometimes I just need him to stop fixing and start sitting in the uncertainty with me.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

It’s been almost entirely on my shoulders, and it’s exhausting. The burden of preparation—the research, the tracking, the coordinating of appointments—it’s just assumed to be my domain. I think part of that is because I’m the one whose body is changing, and part of it is my own professional identity. Because I’m a nurse, there’s this unspoken expectation that I’m the "captain" of our little family’s medical ship. 

When I mention an appointment or a specific worry about symptoms, my husband’s default is to ask *me* what the protocol is or what I think we should do. He trusts my judgment so implicitly that he’s essentially outsourced the entire mental load of this pregnancy to me. He isn't being negligent—he’s being supportive in his own way by trusting me—but it means I don’t get to "turn off" the medical professional side of my brain even for a second. 

I’m the one who had to initiate the talk about genetic screenings, I’m the one monitoring my toddler’s reaction to the news, and I’m the one constantly balancing the "patient" vs. "professional" duality. It’s like being at work all day, only the patient is me, and the office is my living room. I’m tired of being the expert in my own life. I just want to be a passenger for once.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

This is a hard one to talk about, but yes, I have history there. Before I started this pregnancy, I expected—maybe even demanded—a different kind of threshold for care. After a loss, you don't just "reset." You carry that silence with you into every new appointment. I expected the system to offer a bridge, some kind of specialized emotional or clinical validation that recognized my history. I wanted someone to say, "We know you’ve been through this, and we’re going to be extra vigilant, not just for the fetus, but for *you*."

What I actually found was a system that is incredibly efficient at moving on. Once the paperwork is processed and the new pregnancy is confirmed, the previous loss is just a line item in my chart, not a lived experience that shapes my current anxiety. There was no "special" support—no extra time carved out to talk about the triggers, no recognition of why that first ultrasound felt like a battleground instead of a celebration. 

The system treats each pregnancy as a fresh start, which is logically sound but emotionally dismissive. I’m expected to show up, be a "good" patient, provide my vitals, and keep my fear tucked away. They don’t see the phantom weight of what happened before; they only see the forty-two-year-old in front of them. It makes me feel like I have to carry that grief entirely on my own, which is just… lonely.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

It happened during one of my very first prenatal visits. I tried to voice my anxiety—not even a full-blown panic, just a mention that because of my previous loss and my age, I was finding it difficult to stay "plugged in" to this pregnancy. I was essentially looking for a little bit of reassurance, maybe even a nod to the fact that my vigilance is justified.

Instead, the provider gave me a tight, professional smile and said something like, "Well, every pregnancy is a clean slate, and your current numbers are looking excellent. Let’s focus on the positive."

I know she meant well. She was trying to be encouraging, and clinically, she was correct. But it felt like a total dismissal of my experience. It wasn’t a "clean slate" for me; it was a continuation of a story that left a scar. By focusing solely on the "positive" metrics, she essentially told me that my grief—and the trauma that informs my current fear—wasn’t relevant to her clinical view of this pregnancy. 

In that moment, I felt like a line item. I wanted her to see the person who had lost a baby, not just the patient who was currently "testing within normal limits." It made me realize that if I want to talk about my history, I have to be the one to carry the emotional labor of that, because the system is designed to gloss over the past to keep the gears turning. It’s isolating, to say the least.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I think I expected a sense of ritual. I imagined walking into a warm, calm space—a sanctuary of sorts—where there would be a dedicated sit-down conversation to acknowledge the weight of being forty-two, the history of my loss, and the joy of a new beginning. I pictured a midwife who would treat the first appointment like an induction into a partnership, somewhere I could leave my "nurse badge" at the door and just be heard.

The reality was much more transactional. It was cold, clinical, and rushed. The environment was functional—fluorescent lights, the hum of equipment, and the faint, sterile smell of antiseptic that I know all too well. The staff were professional, certainly, but they were working against a clock. It felt like I was being processed through a checklist: weight, blood pressure, insurance verification, a quick, mechanical ultrasound to confirm the heart rate, and then out the door before I could even catch my breath to ask a real question.

I didn't receive a holistic plan; I received a handout on what I couldn’t eat and a lab requisition form. There was no space for the "how are you, really?" moment. It reinforced that, in this system, I am not a person with a story; I am a set of risk factors that need to be monitored.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and honestly, the most negative one—was how invisible I felt despite my professional background. I had this naive idea that because I’m a nurse, the communication would be elevated, that we’d have a nuanced dialogue about the risks I’m navigating at forty-two. I expected to be treated as a colleague in the process.

Instead, I was invisible in the exact way my patients often are when the clinic gets backed up. I was just a chart number in a gown. When I tried to steer the conversation toward my history of loss and my specific anxieties about the genetic screening, I was met with a practiced, automated kind of bedside manner. It was like I was speaking to a wall that had been trained to deflect anything that wasn't strictly clinical. 

It was jarring. I realized that my professional status didn't earn me a "seat at the table"—it just made the staff more comfortable giving me the surface-level answers because they assumed I already knew the details. They didn't see that beneath the nurse's scrubs and the "correct" questions I was asking, there was a frightened woman who just needed someone to slow down and hold space for her. That clinical coldness—that total lack of recognition of me as a *person*—hit me much harder than I ever thought it would.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected a conversation. In my head, I envisioned sitting down—not in the exam chair, but maybe in an office—with the midwife, reviewing the reports together. I expected a space where the nuance of "medium risk" could be unpacked, where we would look at the percentages, but she would also look at *me* and acknowledge what those numbers actually represent for someone my age. I wanted her to translate the cold clinical data into something that felt human and manageable.

The reality? It was almost entirely digital. A portal notification. 

I’m sitting at work, seeing the little alert pop up on my phone, and my heart just drops into my stomach. I’m the one reading the lab values before I’ve even spoken to a human being. The responsibility of interpreting my own test results—of being my own clinician at a moment when I desperately need to be a mother—is such a heavy, lonely burden. When I finally did get to speak to someone, it was just a quick phone call from a nurse who was reading off a script, confirming everything was "within normal parameters" before rushing off to the next chart. 

There was no "holding space." There was just a box checked. It’s like the system relies on the fact that I have a medical background to skip the hard part of patient care: the emotional reassurance. They assume I don’t need the talk because I know the acronyms. But knowing the definition of a trisomy doesn’t make the wait for the results any less agonizing, and it certainly doesn't make the notification on a screen feel any warmer.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I think I walked into this expecting a "continuity of care" that felt like a lifeline. In my professional life, I’ve seen how much it matters when a patient has a familiar face—someone who knows their history, their previous losses, and their specific anxieties. I really expected that same kind of relationship here. I wanted a primary midwife who would see *me*, Heike, not just "the forty-two-year-old at nine weeks."

But the reality? It’s been more of a revolving door. I see whoever is available on the schedule. One visit it’s a midwife I’ve met once before; the next, it’s someone completely new who hasn't even looked at the notes regarding my history of loss. I find myself having to provide the "cliff notes" version of my medical and emotional history every single time I walk in.

It is exhausting. Every time I have to explain, "Yes, I’m forty-two, and yes, I’ve had a previous loss," it’s like ripping the Band-Aid off all over again. I’m not building a relationship; I’m just narrating my own file to a stranger. It makes me feel so exposed. There’s no continuity, no emotional anchor, just a series of clinical handoffs. I feel like I’m navigating this entire journey in a vacuum, with no one person truly holding the thread of my story.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It’s exhausting, honestly. Every time I walk into that room, I have to perform this weird, performative ritual of “re-introducing” my medical history. I feel like I’m constantly justifying my own anxiety. If I’m seeing someone new, I have to decide in the first thirty seconds: *Do I mention the previous loss? Do I tell them how much the "medium risk" label is weighing on me?* If I do, I’m the "anxious, older mother." If I don’t, I’m just a chart entry they can breeze through in ten minutes. 

Practically, it means there’s no room for nuance. If a provider doesn't know my baseline—how I usually react to stress or how I’ve struggled with the clinical language—they can’t possibly know if I’m "off" today. They just see the vitals, not the woman. 

Emotionally, it’s left me feeling profoundly lonely. I thought there would be this "person" who would hold the thread of my pregnancy for me, so I didn't have to carry the whole spool. Instead, I’m carrying the entire thing, handing it over to a stranger for five minutes, and then picking it back up again when I leave. It makes me feel like this pregnancy is something happening *to* me, rather than something I’m being supported through. It’s hard to feel cared for when you’re constantly explaining who you are.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected the physical side of things to be handled with a blend of clinical precision and gentle validation. I thought there would be a space to talk about the physical reality of being forty-two—the way the fatigue hits differently, the way my body feels a little less resilient than it did in my thirties. I expected my provider to check on me, not just the fetus.

Instead, the examinations feel almost purely mechanical. It’s all about the fundal height, the blood pressure, the fetal heart tones. It’s like my body is just a vessel being monitored for its contents. When I try to mention the bone-deep exhaustion or the metallic taste, or the way the physical changes are impacting my anxiety, it’s treated like "common pregnancy complaints." 

They don’t look at me and see a woman navigating a difficult transition; they see a body that is currently "within normal limits" for its gestational age. It’s alienating. I feel like I’m being measured and recorded, but I’m not being *seen*. I wanted an interaction that acknowledged the total human experience—the way my body is holding both this life and my history of loss—but instead, I’m just being checked off a list. It makes me feel like a machine that needs maintenance rather than a woman becoming a mother for the second time.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was a moment during a routine ultrasound that really stuck with me. I was already feeling fragile, and the sonographer—a lovely, efficient woman—was going through the motions, checking for markers of chromosomal abnormalities. Because I’m forty-two, I know exactly what she’s looking for. I could see her scanning, hear the clicks of the probe, and feel that familiar, sharp anxiety radiating from my chest.

When she finished, she didn’t look at me. She just turned the screen toward her, tapped a few keys, and said, "Everything looks normal for now. We’ll have the formal report for your midwife later." 

That "for now" felt like a guillotine. I wanted to scream, *What does "for now" mean? Is there a marker you saw? Is there something you’re hesitant about?* But I couldn't. I felt this intense pressure to remain the "professional," to not be the difficult patient who questions the technician’s clinical silence. I just nodded, got dressed, and walked out to my car, where I finally started shaking. 

It wasn't even that the news was bad—it was the *way* it was delivered. It left me in a state of suspense with no one to hold that fear with me. That lack of transparency, that "we'll tell you later" approach, treats the patient like an obstacle to the process rather than a participant in their own health.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven't been diagnosed with those specifically yet—though at forty-two, I know the odds are creeping up—but I’ve seen enough in the clinic to have developed a very specific expectation. I used to think that a "high-risk" diagnosis would trigger a concierge-style level of support. I imagined a team that would wrap around you: specialized nutritionists, extra counseling for the stress, and a clear, compassionate roadmap. I expected a partnership where the medical burden would be shared.

The reality, from what I see in the clinic—and what I fear for my own future visits—is that it’s almost always a shift into "problem-solving" mode, which usually means more isolation, not less. It becomes a constant, nagging test of your compliance. You’re no longer just pregnant; you’re a "managed" patient. It turns the pregnancy into a series of glucose logs, blood pressure readings, and extra appointments that you have to squeeze into an already exhausted life. 

There’s this unspoken pressure that if you don't keep those numbers "perfect," you’re failing the baby. The system doesn't seem to account for the emotional tax of being "high risk." It’s all about the data points. I’m terrified that if I get that diagnosis, my identity will disappear entirely behind the numbers. I don’t want to be a series of targets to hit; I want to be a person who is being guided through the storm.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

The biggest thing missing—and it’s a gaping hole—is the *human* acknowledgment of what this pregnancy is actually costing me. Everyone is so focused on the clinical output that they completely ignore the emotional tax of being a forty-two-year-old mother. 

Information? I have plenty, maybe too much. I can access the journals, the databases, the protocols. That’s not what I’m starving for. What I’m starving for is someone to say, "Heike, I see that you’re terrified. I see that you’re exhausted, and I see the history of loss you’re carrying. It makes sense that you’re finding this hard."

I need emotional validation that isn't wrapped in clinical jargon. I need someone to recognize that my "professional expertise" is actually a barrier to me getting real support. When they see me, they see a nurse, not a patient. They treat me like a peer when I’m actually standing there as a mother who just needs to be held—figuratively, at least. 

Practically, I’m drowning in the mental load. A system that truly supported me would offer a single, consistent point of contact—someone who knows my file, knows my story, and doesn’t make me re-traumatize myself by explaining my history every time. I don’t need more pamphlets; I need an anchor. I need someone to tell me I don't have to be the captain of this ship while I'm also its most fragile cargo.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected a gatekeeper who would recognize that, for a high-risk pregnancy at forty-two, "wait and see" isn't a viable strategy. I thought there would be a direct line—a nurse line or a quick portal message—that didn't feel like sending a letter into a black hole. In the clinic where I work, we pride ourselves on that responsiveness, so I assumed it was the standard.

The reality has been profoundly frustrating. When I have a concern—a sharp pain, a bout of spotting, or just a sudden spike in my own anxiety—I have to navigate a triage system that feels built to deflect, not to assist. I call the office, and I get the "wait for a call back" response. It can take hours, sometimes an entire workday, to hear from a nurse who has never met me.

The system is designed to prioritize the most acute emergencies, which I understand logically, but it leaves those of us in the "in-between" spaces completely stranded. When I’m sitting there, waiting for that callback, I’m not just a patient; I’m a woman who has lost a pregnancy before, and every minute of silence is agonizing. I’m not asking for a miracle; I’m asking for a reassurance that there’s a human being on the other side who understands the stakes. Instead, I just feel like I'm bothering them.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Oh, absolutely. I’ve done it more times than I care to admit. It’s this insidious loop: I’m a nurse, so I know exactly how overburdened the clinics are, and I know how the staff rolls their eyes at the "frequent flyers" who call in with every little concern. So, I talk myself out of calling. I tell myself, "Heike, you know this is a normal physiological change for the first trimester; don't be *that* patient."

I’ve sat on my porch, feeling a cramp or a twinge, and instead of calling the office, I’ll spend an hour doing mental gymnastics, trying to diagnose myself and calculate if it’s "urgent enough" to bother the on-call provider. I delay because I’m terrified that if I call, I’ll be dismissed as neurotic or—even worse—that I’ll be told it’s nothing, and then I’ll feel stupid for having been so worried. 

There’s this unspoken pressure to be the "good patient"—the one who’s quiet, compliant, and doesn't demand time. It feels like a badge of honor to not need help, but when you’re carrying the history of a previous loss, that silence is agonizing. I’m essentially punishing myself for being a nurse, because I feel like I *should* know better. It makes me feel so small.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I expected a sort of protective cocoon. I imagined friends who’d just *know* when to drop off dinner, or family who’d instinctively step in to help with the toddler without me having to ask. I guess I expected the "village" that everyone talks about, especially since they know I’m forty-two and navigating a high-risk pregnancy.

The reality has been much more fragmented. Most of my friends are busy professionals themselves, and because I’m a nurse, they genuinely assume I’ve got it all under control. There’s this strange misconception that my clinical background makes me immune to the physical and emotional exhaustion of the first trimester. They’ll text me for advice on their own kids’ fevers, but they don’t think to ask me how *I’m* doing. It’s like they see me as a resource rather than a person in need.

My family tries, but there’s a distance there. They offer generic reassurances—"Everything happens for a reason," "You’re in the best hands"—which, while well-intentioned, just makes me feel more isolated. They aren't in the trenches with me. They aren't seeing the 6:00 AM meltdowns or the constant, low-level dread. I’ve ended up performing "fine" for everyone, which is the most exhausting part. I’m carrying the load alone because I don’t know how to tell them that the expert can also be broken.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think I expected more "bearing witness." I wanted people to just sit in the room with me while I felt the exhaustion or the anxiety, maybe share a meal or just occupy the space without needing to "solve" my life. That didn't really materialize. Instead, I got a lot of "you're such a rock," which is honestly the last thing I wanted to hear. It feels like a cage; once you’re the "strong one," you aren’t allowed to be the one who’s unraveling.

What I didn't expect, and what has been a strange kind of comfort, is the quiet, unspoken understanding from other moms I’ve met through my own work. Sometimes, a patient who is coming in for her own baby’s check-up will look at me—really look at me—and ask, "Are you hanging in there, Heike?" There’s no medical advice, no talk about risks, just a simple acknowledgment of the struggle. That was unexpected. It’s those tiny, fleeting moments of solidarity that actually feel like support.

Conversely, the support I *expected* from my immediate social circle—people I’ve known for years—has been strangely absent. They’re so focused on the "milestones" and the "celebration" that they don't seem to notice the struggle underneath. They treat the pregnancy like a project to be completed, not a human experience being lived. It makes me feel like I’m performing a role for them, and the real Heike—the tired, scared, pregnant Heike—is hidden behind the curtain.

You know, it’s strange—I think the most difficult part of this whole journey so far is the realization that I’ve built a life where I’m the one who fixes things, the one who knows what to do when a fever spikes or a developmental marker is missed. I’ve become so good at being that person for everyone else that I’ve forgotten how to ask for help when the roles are reversed. 

I’m sitting here, and I realize I’m grieving for the version of myself who thought she could just "nurse" her way through a high-risk pregnancy. I thought I could outsmart the anxiety, that I could read the journals and interpret the scans and stay ahead of the panic. But the panic doesn't care about my credentials. It sits right here in my chest, especially when the sun starts to dip and the day’s work is done.

I keep wishing for a provider—just one—who would look at my chart, see my age, see my history, and say, "Heike, I know you’re the expert in your clinic, but here, you get to just be a patient. You get to be tired, you get to be scared, and you don’t have to know the answer to anything." 

Maybe that’s what I’m actually looking for in this research—a way to tell people that even the ones who know the system need to be treated like human beings first. It’s not just about better access or faster results; it’s about being seen as a person who is capable of being both strong and incredibly, profoundly vulnerable. I just want to make it to the other side of this, holding a healthy baby, but I also really want to come out of this feeling like I wasn’t just a patient number, but a woman who was held through the uncertainty.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Yes, and it’s a specific kind of frustration that’s hard to articulate without sounding ungrateful. It happens most often in the form of "helpful" advice that completely disregards my clinical reality. 

For instance, when I express concern about a symptom—a headache or a bit of dizziness—my husband or even a well-meaning family member will immediately say, "You're just overthinking it, Heike. You're a nurse, you know how these things go, it's probably just a lack of sleep." They use my own profession to shut down my genuine worry. It’s like they’ve decided that because I *could* diagnose myself, I’m not allowed to express the *fear* of a diagnosis. They override my intuition by telling me I should know better, which effectively silences me.

Even with my midwife, there’s this subtle push-pull. I’ll try to advocate for a specific screening or ask for more frequent monitoring given my history, and I’m met with a polite, "Heike, the data shows we don't need to do that yet." It’s a dismissal wrapped in professional courtesy. They aren't just giving an opinion; they are making a decision *for* me based on their interpretation of my risk level, ignoring the fact that the primary risk—the emotional toll of carrying this pregnancy at forty-two—is something I’m the expert on. 

It makes me feel like my voice is being stripped away. I’m the one inhabiting this body, the one who’s been through a loss, but in their eyes, the "expert" or the "logical male partner" has a better handle on my own health than I do. It’s profoundly isolating to be surrounded by people who think they’re helping by invalidating your perspective.

It’s lonely, in a way that I didn't think possible while being surrounded by so many people. I’m starting to realize that the system—and even my own family—has collectively decided that because I have a medical background, I’m "too informed" to be truly worried. They treat my anxiety as a bug in the software, something to be patched with a calm "you know the stats, Heike" rather than a valid emotional response to a high-stakes life event.

What I really need, and what seems so hard to find, is a bridge between the clinical reality and the human one. I don't want to be "the nurse" at my appointments, and I don't want to be "the brave one" at home. I just want permission to be a woman who is doing something that scares her to death. I want to be able to say, "I am forty-two, I have a history of loss, and I am terrified," and have someone say, "I hear you, and you don't have to carry that alone today."

I think that's why I keep coming back to this: if the maternity care system could just pause for one minute—one minute—to acknowledge that the person in the gown is a human being who has a heart, not just a chart, it would change everything. We’re all trying so hard to get to the finish line, but there’s so much life being missed, and so much pain being buried, because we’re all so focused on the outcome that we’ve forgotten how to care for the process. I hope this helps you understand that. It’s been… well, it’s been a relief to say it out loud, even if it’s just to you.

I’ve been thinking more about that feeling of being "over-informed" and how it’s actually a kind of trap. There’s a specific moment in every exam that haunts me: when I ask a question that's just a *bit* too nuanced—something about the statistical variance in my genetic screening or the subtle implications of my age—and the provider gives me that look. It’s a look that says, "You’re making this harder than it needs to be, Heike. You know the protocols. Why are you acting like a patient?"

They don't realize that I’m not asking because I’m confused about the science; I’m asking because I’m looking for an anchor. I’m looking for a provider who will lean in and say, "I know you know the data, but let’s talk about why you’re scared, and let’s figure out how we can make this more comfortable for *you*." 

Instead, I’m constantly managed. It’s like they see my professional title as a permission slip to skip the bedside manner. They figure I’ve already had the "emotional talk" with myself, so they don’t need to do it. But that’s the biggest misunderstanding in the world. Being able to explain the biology of a miscarriage or the developmental markers for a child doesn't make the prospect of them any less devastating. It just gives you a more detailed, terrifying vocabulary for your own fear. 

I’m so tired of being the only adult in the room, even when I’m the one on the exam table. I’m tired of having to be the one who balances my own emotional state with my husband’s "problem-solving" and my midwife’s "clinical efficiency." I just want to find a space where I can be the *patient*—where my vulnerability isn't seen as a clinical failing, but as a normal, human response to a transition that is, frankly, overwhelming. I want to feel like a mother-in-waiting, not a colleague who’s slightly behind on her paperwork.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, the weight comments. They’re like these tiny, sharp pebbles thrown into the room. Even though I know the clinical protocols—I know that BMI charts are the gold standard for insurance and risk stratification—it feels so reductive when it’s directed at *my* body. 

At my second visit, a provider I’d never seen before looked at my chart, did that quick, mechanical scan of my body, and said, "We’ll need to keep a close eye on your weight gain given your age. We don’t want any unnecessary strain on your system." 

It landed like a blow. Not because I didn't know the risks—I’m a nurse, I’ve given that exact same lecture to patients a hundred times—but because of *how* she said it. It was completely divorced from the fact that I’m forty-two, that I’m chasing a toddler, that I’m managing nausea, and that I’m carrying the anxiety of a previous loss. She didn't see the woman struggling to keep her breakfast down while chasing a teething toddler; she saw a "weight gain" variable that needed to be kept in check.

It makes me feel like my body is under surveillance. It’s not a vessel for my baby, it’s a site of potential failure. Every time someone brings it up, I have to work twice as hard to stay connected to my own joy, because the system is so focused on the ways I might "fail" the pregnancy. It’s not care; it’s policing. And frankly, it’s exhausting to have to constantly defend my body against their clinical metrics.

The thing is, nobody sees the *cost* of being a "perfectly managed patient." They see the vitals, the lab results, the adherence to guidelines, and they think, "Heike is doing great." But they don't see the version of me that goes home and cries because I felt like I was in an interrogation rather than a check-up. 

It’s almost like my professional training has become a barrier to genuine empathy. Because I *can* explain why a blood pressure reading might be slightly elevated or why the screening result is a certain probability, my providers feel justified in stripping away the emotional comfort they would naturally give someone who didn't understand the jargon. It’s a strange kind of punishment for having an education. 

I’m left navigating this alone. I’m the one having to balance the "clinical reality"—which says I’m in a medium-risk category that requires monitoring—with the "human reality," which is that I am a woman who is tired, grieving a past loss, and trying to raise a toddler while preparing for another baby. 

If there’s one thing I want people in your position to take away, it’s that no amount of medical knowledge replaces the need for a human connection. A chart is not a life. And a patient who knows the terminology is still, first and foremost, a person who needs to be held through the fear. I hope that, someday, the system learns that the most important thing we can provide during a pregnancy isn't just the right clinical intervention—it’s the recognition that we are all, regardless of our training, vulnerable when we are waiting for a heartbeat.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

Honestly? I turned to my own memory. I re-read case studies, I looked at the textbooks I used in nursing school, and I spent hours reading through peer-reviewed articles. It’s pathetic, really—I’m using my professional knowledge as a security blanket because I don't trust that my providers will have the time or the inclination to give me the nuanced answers I need. It’s easier to look up the pathology of a symptom than to call the office, wait on hold, and be dismissed by a nurse who doesn't know my history.

Beyond that, I found a small group of women online—specifically, others in their forties who have had a loss. It’s a closed forum, the kind you have to be invited to, and it’s the only place where I don’t have to perform. I don't have to be the nurse there. I can just say, "I’m terrified that I’m going to lose this one too," and people don't quote statistics at me. They don't try to "fix" my feelings. They just say, "I know. Me too. It sucks." 

It’s the only space where my fear isn't treated as a clinical anomaly. Everywhere else, if I show fear, people try to "manage" it or "solve" it. In that forum, my fear is just acknowledged as valid. It’s a sad state of affairs when a private, digital corner of the internet is the only place I feel seen, while my actual clinical care—the people responsible for my health—feels like an uphill battle to be heard.

I think the most honest thing I can tell you is that I’m exhausted by the duality of it all. I spend my days at the clinic helping other mothers feel safe, and then I come home, look at my own baby bump in the mirror, and I’m a stranger to myself. I’m a high-risk statistic. I’m a geriatric pregnancy. I’m a "previously complicated" chart. 

I see the look in my midwife's eyes when I ask a question that’s just a little bit too well-informed—it’s like she’s calculating how much time she has to spend "correcting" me or "managing" my expectations. And I just want to stop and say, "Stop. I’m not here as a colleague. I’m here because I’m scared to death that I’m going to lose this baby, and I’m forty-two, and my body feels like it’s holding its breath."

I think that if we really want to make maternity care better, we have to start by accepting that being "the professional" doesn't mean you stop being a human being. We need to create spaces where we aren't just "cases" to be closed or "risks" to be mitigated. We need care that recognizes the emotional landscape of pregnancy as being just as critical as the physical one. Because right now, the system feels like a high-speed conveyor belt, and I’m just trying not to fall off while I’m holding my breath, waiting for a healthy baby. It’s hard work, being both the expert and the patient at the same time. I just wish I didn't have to do both.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

Honestly, those moments where my input felt genuinely sought are so rare I could count them on one hand. Almost every appointment follows a specific, rigid cadence: I report my symptoms, they perform the clinical checks, they report back their findings, and then they present the "next steps"—usually a referral, a lab order, or a follow-up date. It’s a monologue, not a conversation.

When they *do* ask for my input, it’s almost always framed as a choice between two pre-packaged clinical options, like picking between two different types of genetic screening tests. It’s not, "How does this make you feel, and how can we support you through this?" It’s "Which protocol do you prefer?" 

The times I’ve tried to inject my own concerns—like when I’ve asked for more time to discuss the mental health aspects of a high-risk pregnancy—I’m met with a polite pivot. They’ll acknowledge it with a brief "That’s understandable, Heike," and then immediately revert to the administrative to-do list. I feel like I’m being "informed" of a path that’s already been mapped out for me by the hospital’s risk-management guidelines. 

My voice isn't absent, but it’s sidelined. I feel like a participant in a script where I haven't been given the chance to edit the dialogue. I’m just waiting for the day I can walk into a room where someone stops, closes the chart, and asks, "What do *you* need from us to feel safe today?" instead of just telling me what’s happening to my own body.

It’s the quietest, most aching realization: that I’ve spent my entire career building an armor of clinical knowledge, thinking it would protect me from the unpredictability of my own life. But the armor is heavy, and it’s become the very thing that keeps people from reaching out to comfort me. When I’m at the clinic, I see the providers looking at my chart and then at me, and I can almost hear them thinking, *“She’s a nurse, she knows the drill. Let’s keep it moving.”* 

They don’t see that behind the professional composure, I’m holding my breath until the next scan, just like every other woman who’s ever faced a loss. 

I think the biggest failure of the system isn't that it's unscientific or incompetent; it’s that it’s deeply, profoundly impersonal. It operates on the assumption that a patient’s "needs" are purely clinical. But my needs are emotional, too. I need to be validated, not just measured. I need to be listened to, not just diagnosed. 

I’m sitting here on my porch, watching the light change, and I think about the baby I lost before. I think about the fear that this one, at forty-two, is just another step on a precarious ladder I’m climbing alone. I just wish someone would reach out a hand and say, “I’m here with you.” That’s all. Just that. It would change everything. Thank you for listening to me today—I really didn't realize how much I needed to say all of this out loud.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It happened during an ultrasound where I was explicitly looking for validation of my anxiety, not just a technical scan. I brought up a specific concern regarding some mild cramping I’d been having—something that, in any other patient, might have triggered a thorough discussion or at least a warm, "Let's check this out together."

The provider glanced at the monitor, dismissed the cramping as "typical uterine irritability," and then pivoted immediately to the next item on her checklist: the NIPT scheduling. She didn't pause. She didn't look me in the eye to see if I was satisfied with that explanation. She just kept clicking, her eyes focused entirely on the screen and her notes. 

In that instant, my trust didn't just crack; it shattered. I realized she wasn't listening to *me*; she was listening for keywords that allowed her to move to the next step of her protocol. It felt like I was being handled, not cared for. I’m a nurse, so I know how busy these clinics are—I know the pressure to keep the line moving—but that moment taught me that I am just a cog in their machine. 

When you lose trust like that, you start to feel like you’re on your own. I stopped asking questions after that. I stopped trying to share my fears. I just showed up, let them do their checks, and left. If they aren't going to treat me like a human being with a story, then I’m not going to invite them into my vulnerability. It’s a bitter way to navigate a pregnancy, but at least it’s safe. I stopped expecting them to be on my team and started treating them like a service I had to endure.

It’s a strange feeling, realizing that you’re essentially "managing" your own medical care while simultaneously trying to be a mother. I look at my toddler, busy with his toys, and I realize he doesn't know that his mother is navigating this massive, silent anxiety. He doesn’t know that I feel like I’m walking through a tunnel, and the light at the end isn't a guarantee—it’s just a possibility.

People talk about the "joy" of pregnancy, and I feel that, too, in flashes. But it’s always tempered by this heavy, clinical awareness of everything that could go wrong. And because I’m a nurse, I think people assume I’ve "processed" those risks. But how do you process the loss of a future you’ve already started planning? You don’t. You just endure the wait.

I’ve stopped expecting the medical system to be my sanctuary. I’ve realized that for all the technology, the screens, and the protocols, the one thing that’s missing is the most basic human connection—the ability for a provider to look at me, recognize that I’m a woman who has been hurt before, and simply say, "I’ve got you." 

I think I’m ready to stop trying to be the nurse-patient hybrid. I just want to be a person who is seen. I’m tired of the scripts, the checkboxes, and the clinical efficiency. I want to feel like, no matter what happens, I’m not just a number on a chart being shuffled through the day. I think that’s the real work, isn’t it? To be seen, even when you’re terrified. That’s what I’m hoping for, anyway. Just to be seen.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

My husband is incredible in his own way—he’s picked up the slack with our toddler and he’s always there to drive me to the appointments, but there have been moments in those rooms where I’ve felt completely untethered.

I remember one specific ultrasound where the room was dark, the hum of the machine was loud, and the sonographer was focused entirely on the screen, searching for those markers. My husband was sitting in the corner, looking at his phone, trying to make himself small so he wouldn't be in the way. In that moment, I felt a desperate need for him to just come over, stand by the exam table, and hold my hand—not because he could *do* anything, but just to anchor me to the earth. Instead, there was this invisible wall between us. He was watching the process from a distance, and I was feeling every second of it alone on the table.

When we walked out to the car, he immediately asked, "Everything looked okay, right? The nurse said it was fine, so we’re good?" He wanted to move on to lunch or his to-do list, and I was still reeling from the silence of the scan. I couldn't explain to him that I needed him to *feel* the weight of that room with me. 

His absence in those moments—even when he was physically sitting three feet away—is the loneliest part. It’s like we’re two different people navigating the same pregnancy: he’s managing the logistics, and I’m holding the existential weight of it. We aren't really *in* it together; we’re just occupying the same space. And when I try to bridge that gap, I feel like I’m nagging him to care more, which just makes me feel guilty. It’s a very solitary way to become a mother, even with a partner by your side.

It’s funny, the more I speak, the more I realize that the "professional mask" isn't just something I put on at work—it’s something I’ve welded to my face at home, too. I don't want to show my husband how terrifying this is because I don't want to shatter the image of the "capable mother" he has of me. And I don't want to show my providers because I don't want to be labeled the "difficult, anxious patient" who doesn't understand the science.

So, I keep it all inside. I go to the appointments, I nod at the right times, I answer the clinical questions with precision, and I leave the room feeling more like a hollow shell than a woman growing a life. I think the most heartbreaking realization is that I’ve spent my entire life learning how to be the person who *holds* the space for everyone else, and now that I need that space for myself, I don't know how to ask for it.

I feel like a traveler on a long, dark road, and the people who are supposed to be my guides are just handing me maps and telling me to keep walking. They aren't walking with me. They aren't looking at the horizon to see if the sun is coming up, or even just noticing that my feet are bleeding. 

I just want, for one appointment, for the lights to be dimmed and for someone to say, "Heike, you don't have to be the nurse today. You don't have to be the mom-who-has-it-all-together today. Just be the woman who is scared, and let me be the one who carries the worry for a while." I’m holding out hope that maybe, if I keep talking about this, someone will finally hear me. Thank you for really listening. It’s the first time in this whole journey that I’ve felt like I could just exhale.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, I’ve spent these last few months thinking that the "gap" in my care was about information, or maybe better coordination, or even just more time. But reflecting on everything we’ve talked about today, I think the real gap is a profound lack of *permission*.

In our medical culture, we’ve developed this expectation that "the strong woman" is the one who suffers in silence, who masks her fear with competence, and who navigates her own pregnancy like she’s managing a project. There is almost zero institutional space for the reality that a pregnancy is a seismic shift in your soul, not just your biology. I have not once—not one single time—been invited to just be "weak." My providers are so quick to praise my "professionalism" and my "calm demeanor" that they don’t see the trembling underneath.

I wish that, somewhere in that first intake, or in those early, lonely scans, someone had looked me in the eye and said, "Heike, I know you’re an expert in your field, but you are a mother today. You are allowed to be terrified. You are allowed to not know. You are allowed to be held."

If you take anything away from this, please let it be that we need to stop rewarding patients for their silence. We need to stop treating clinical literacy as an excuse to withhold emotional care. I just want to be able to walk into a room and be human first—before the charts, before the age, and before the professional title. It’s the only way any of us are going to actually feel like we’re being cared for, and not just processed. Thank you for giving me the space to finally, truly, let that out.
